---
title: "Granger causality in strategic interactions"
description: "Test for Granger causality between strategic variables in game-theoretic settings, using VAR estimation, F-tests, and impulse response analysis to determine whether one firm's pricing decisions predictively lead another's."
author: "Raban Heller"
date: 2026-05-08
date-modified: 2026-05-08
categories:
  - time-series-econometrics
  - Granger causality
  - VAR models
  - strategic pricing
keywords: ["Granger causality", "vector autoregression", "strategic interaction", "time series", "R"]
labels: ["econometrics", "time-series"]
tier: 1
bibliography: ../../references.bib
vgwort: "TODO_VGWORT_time-series-econometrics_granger-causality-strategic"
image: thumbnail.png
image-alt: "Time series plot showing two firms pricing trajectories with impulse response functions indicating Granger-causal relationships"
citation:
  type: webpage
  url: https://r-heller.github.io/equilibria/tutorials/time-series-econometrics/granger-causality-strategic/
license: "CC BY-SA 4.0"
draft: false
has_static_fig: true
has_interactive_fig: true
has_shiny_app: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(plotly)
okabe_ito <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#F0E442",
               "#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7", "#999999")
theme_publication <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = base_size * 1.2, face = "bold"),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = base_size * 0.9, color = "grey40"),
          axis.line = element_line(color = "grey30", linewidth = 0.3),
          panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom",
          plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10))
}
```

## Introduction & motivation

In markets where firms interact repeatedly, a fundamental empirical question arises: does one firm's strategic behavior influence another's future decisions? Standard game theory models simultaneous decision-making through Nash equilibrium concepts, but real markets unfold in continuous time, with firms observing each other's past actions and adjusting accordingly. A gasoline station might lower its price after observing a competitor's price cut yesterday. An airline might adjust seat availability on a route after seeing a rival's fare changes last week. These temporal patterns of strategic response are precisely what Granger causality tests are designed to detect.

Granger causality, introduced by Clive Granger in 1969, formalizes a specific notion of predictive precedence: a variable $X$ Granger-causes variable $Y$ if past values of $X$ contain information useful for predicting $Y$ beyond what is already contained in past values of $Y$ itself. This is not causality in the structural or interventionist sense -- it is a statement about predictive information content in time series data. Nevertheless, in strategic settings, Granger causality provides valuable evidence about the direction and timing of competitive responses.

The application of Granger causality to strategic interactions raises several methodological challenges that distinguish it from standard macroeconomic applications. First, if firms truly play a simultaneous-move game each period, their actions are determined by common state variables rather than by each other's past actions, and Granger causality tests may fail to detect genuine strategic interdependence. Second, if firms observe each other's actions with a lag and respond optimally, the pattern of Granger causality reflects the information structure and adjustment speed rather than the underlying payoff structure. Third, common shocks -- such as input cost changes or demand shifts -- can induce spurious Granger causality between firms' strategies if not properly controlled for.

Despite these caveats, Granger causality analysis within a Vector Autoregression (VAR) framework offers practical value for applied researchers studying competitive dynamics. The VAR model treats all strategic variables symmetrically, estimating how each variable depends on its own lags and the lags of all other variables. Impulse response functions derived from the estimated VAR show how a shock to one firm's strategy propagates through the system over time, capturing the dynamic multiplier effects of strategic adjustment.

In this tutorial, we simulate a duopoly pricing game where two firms set prices over 300 periods, with firm A acting as a Stackelberg-like leader whose prices Granger-cause firm B's, while firm B's prices have a weaker reverse effect. We estimate a VAR model, conduct Granger causality F-tests, and compute impulse response functions to visualize the dynamic strategic interaction.

## Mathematical formulation

Consider a bivariate VAR($p$) model for two firms' prices $(P_t^A, P_t^B)$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} P_t^A \\ P_t^B \end{pmatrix} = \boldsymbol{c} + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{A}_k \begin{pmatrix} P_{t-k}^A \\ P_{t-k}^B \end{pmatrix} + \boldsymbol{u}_t
$$

where $\boldsymbol{A}_k$ are $2 \times 2$ coefficient matrices and $\boldsymbol{u}_t \sim N(\boldsymbol{0}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$. The null hypothesis for Granger non-causality from $P^A$ to $P^B$ is:

$$
H_0: A_k^{(2,1)} = 0 \quad \forall k = 1, \ldots, p
$$

The F-test statistic under the null is:

$$
F = \frac{(RSS_R - RSS_U) / p}{RSS_U / (T - 2p - 1)} \sim F(p, T - 2p - 1)
$$

The impulse response function at horizon $h$ is given by the $(i,j)$ element of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_h$, computed recursively:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Phi}_h = \sum_{k=1}^{h} \boldsymbol{A}_k \boldsymbol{\Phi}_{h-k}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\Phi}_0 = \boldsymbol{I}_2
$$

## R implementation

We simulate duopoly pricing data with asymmetric Granger-causal structure and estimate the VAR model using OLS.

```{r}
#| label: granger-estimation

set.seed(42)

n <- 300
p_a <- numeric(n)
p_b <- numeric(n)
p_a[1:2] <- c(10, 10.2)
p_b[1:2] <- c(10, 9.8)

for (t in 3:n) {
  p_a[t] <- 2 + 0.6 * p_a[t-1] + 0.1 * p_a[t-2] +
            0.05 * p_b[t-1] + rnorm(1, 0, 0.3)
  p_b[t] <- 1.5 + 0.4 * p_b[t-1] + 0.1 * p_b[t-2] +
            0.3 * p_a[t-1] + 0.1 * p_a[t-2] + rnorm(1, 0, 0.3)
}

df_ts <- data.frame(t = 1:n, price_A = p_a, price_B = p_b)

lag1_a <- p_a[2:(n-1)]; lag2_a <- p_a[1:(n-2)]
lag1_b <- p_b[2:(n-1)]; lag2_b <- p_b[1:(n-2)]
y_a <- p_a[3:n]; y_b <- p_b[3:n]

full_b <- lm(y_b ~ lag1_b + lag2_b + lag1_a + lag2_a)
rest_b <- lm(y_b ~ lag1_b + lag2_b)

f_a_to_b <- ((sum(rest_b$residuals^2) - sum(full_b$residuals^2)) / 2) /
             (sum(full_b$residuals^2) / full_b$df.residual)
p_val_a_to_b <- pf(f_a_to_b, 2, full_b$df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE)

full_a <- lm(y_a ~ lag1_a + lag2_a + lag1_b + lag2_b)
rest_a <- lm(y_a ~ lag1_a + lag2_a)

f_b_to_a <- ((sum(rest_a$residuals^2) - sum(full_a$residuals^2)) / 2) /
             (sum(full_a$residuals^2) / full_a$df.residual)
p_val_b_to_a <- pf(f_b_to_a, 2, full_a$df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE)

cat("Granger causality test results:\n")
cat(sprintf("  A -> B: F = %.3f, p-value = %.6f %s\n",
            f_a_to_b, p_val_a_to_b,
            ifelse(p_val_a_to_b < 0.05, "(significant)", "(not significant)")))
cat(sprintf("  B -> A: F = %.3f, p-value = %.6f %s\n",
            f_b_to_a, p_val_b_to_a,
            ifelse(p_val_b_to_a < 0.05, "(significant)", "(not significant)")))

# Compute impulse responses via simulation
A1 <- matrix(c(coef(full_a)["lag1_a"], coef(full_b)["lag1_a"],
               coef(full_a)["lag1_b"], coef(full_b)["lag1_b"]), 2, 2)
A2 <- matrix(c(coef(full_a)["lag2_a"], coef(full_b)["lag2_a"],
               coef(full_a)["lag2_b"], coef(full_b)["lag2_b"]), 2, 2)

horizons <- 20
irf_a_shock <- matrix(0, horizons + 1, 2)
irf_a_shock[1, ] <- c(1, 0)
if (horizons >= 1) irf_a_shock[2, ] <- A1 %*% irf_a_shock[1, ]
for (h in 3:(horizons + 1)) {
  irf_a_shock[h, ] <- A1 %*% irf_a_shock[h-1, ] + A2 %*% irf_a_shock[h-2, ]
}

irf_df <- data.frame(
  horizon = rep(0:horizons, 2),
  response = c(irf_a_shock[, 1], irf_a_shock[, 2]),
  variable = rep(c("Price A (own response)", "Price B (cross response)"),
                 each = horizons + 1)
)

cat(sprintf("\nImpulse response of B to A shock at horizon 1: %.4f\n",
            irf_a_shock[2, 2]))
cat(sprintf("Impulse response of B to A shock at horizon 5: %.4f\n",
            irf_a_shock[6, 2]))
```

## Static publication-ready figure

The static figure displays the impulse response functions showing how a unit shock to Firm A's price propagates to both firms' prices over time.

```{r}
#| label: fig-granger-static
#| fig-cap: "Figure 1. Impulse response functions from a unit shock to Firm A's price in a bivariate VAR(2) model of duopoly pricing. The blue line shows Firm A's own price adjustment, while the orange line traces the cross-response of Firm B's price, revealing the asymmetric Granger-causal structure where A's pricing decisions propagate strongly to B. Simulated data, 300 periods. Okabe-Ito palette."
#| dev: [png, pdf]
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5
#| dpi: 300

p_static <- ggplot(irf_df, aes(x = horizon, y = response, color = variable)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(okabe_ito[2], okabe_ito[1]),
                     name = "Response") +
  labs(
    title = "Impulse response to a shock in Firm A's price",
    subtitle = "VAR(2) model of strategic duopoly pricing",
    x = "Horizon (periods)",
    y = "Response magnitude"
  ) +
  theme_publication()

p_static
```

## Interactive figure

The interactive version allows precise inspection of impulse response values at each horizon through hover tooltips.

```{r}
#| label: fig-granger-interactive

irf_interactive <- irf_df |>
  mutate(
    text = paste0(
      "Horizon: ", horizon,
      "\nResponse: ", sprintf("%.4f", response),
      "\nVariable: ", variable
    )
  )

p_int <- ggplot(irf_interactive, aes(x = horizon, y = response,
                                      color = variable, text = text)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(okabe_ito[2], okabe_ito[1]),
                     name = "Response") +
  labs(title = "Interactive IRF explorer",
       x = "Horizon", y = "Response") +
  theme_publication()

ggplotly(p_int, tooltip = "text") |>
  config(displaylogo = FALSE,
         modeBarButtonsToRemove = c("select2d", "lasso2d"))
```

## Interpretation

The Granger causality test results confirm the asymmetric strategic relationship embedded in our simulated data. The test for A Granger-causing B yields a highly significant F-statistic, reflecting the strong coefficients (0.3 on the first lag, 0.1 on the second lag) by which Firm B responds to Firm A's past prices. In contrast, the reverse test -- B Granger-causing A -- produces a much weaker result, consistent with the small coefficient (0.05) by which Firm A responds to Firm B. This asymmetry is characteristic of markets with a price leader: the leader sets price based primarily on its own cost and demand conditions, while the follower tracks the leader's price closely.

The impulse response functions provide a dynamic view of this asymmetry that goes beyond the binary significant/not-significant conclusion of the F-test. After a unit shock to Firm A's price, Firm B's price responds positively and substantially in the first period, then gradually decays as the system returns to its steady state. The speed of this decay reflects the stability properties of the VAR system -- the eigenvalues of the companion matrix determine whether shocks are absorbed quickly or persist. In our simulation, the moderate autoregressive coefficients ensure that shocks dissipate within approximately 10 to 15 periods, which in a real market might correspond to several weeks or months depending on the frequency of price adjustment.

Several important caveats apply when interpreting Granger causality in strategic settings. First, Granger causality is a statement about predictive content, not structural causation. If both firms respond to a common unobserved demand shock with different lags, Granger causality tests might indicate that the faster-responding firm "causes" the slower one, even though both are responding to the same exogenous force. In applied work, including control variables for observable common factors (input costs, demand indicators, macroeconomic conditions) helps mitigate this concern, though it cannot fully eliminate it.

Second, the lag structure of the VAR matters critically. Too few lags may miss genuine delayed responses, leading to false negatives. Too many lags reduce statistical power and may introduce spurious significance. Information criteria such as AIC or BIC should guide lag selection, with robustness checks across neighboring lag orders. In our implementation, we used two lags matching the true data-generating process, but in practice the researcher does not know the true lag structure.

Third, and most fundamentally, if firms play a genuinely simultaneous game each period -- choosing prices at the same instant without observing each other's current-period choice -- then the strategic interaction is captured entirely in the contemporaneous correlation of the error terms, not in the lagged coefficients. Granger causality tests, which examine only lagged relationships, will fail to detect this form of strategic interdependence. Structural VAR methods, which impose identifying restrictions to decompose contemporaneous correlations, are needed to address this case. The Cholesky decomposition commonly used for identification implicitly assumes a causal ordering that may or may not align with the true timing of decisions.

## Extensions & related tutorials

Granger causality analysis in strategic settings connects naturally to broader topics in time-series econometrics and game-theoretic empirical methods. Extending the bivariate VAR to larger systems allows analysis of multi-firm oligopolies, while structural identification strategies can help distinguish genuine strategic causation from common-factor effects.

- [Bootstrap methods for game-theoretic inference](../../statistical-foundations/bootstrap-methods-game-theory/)
- [Airbnb host pricing as a strategic game](../../real-world-data-applications/airbnb-pricing-strategy/)
- [Agent-based modeling for game theory in R](../../simulations/agent-based-modeling-netlogo-style/)
- [Interactive dashboards for game theory with flexdashboard](../../visualization-and-communication/dashboard-flexdashboard-games/)

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
